article.disclaimerText: This article is educational content about search funds and Entrepreneurship Through Acquisition (ETA). It does not constitute financial, legal, tax, or investment advice. Always consult qualified professional advisors before making investment or acquisition decisions.
article.authorBio: Our editorial team combines academic research from Stanford GSB, INSEAD, IESE, and HEC with practitioner insights to produce the most comprehensive ETA knowledge base in Europe.

journey.title: From Corporate Career to CEO: Your Complete ETA Journey
journey.subtitle: A step-by-step visual guide through every phase of acquiring and operating a business. Built for searchers, investors, and sellers.
journey.outroTitle: Ready to start your ETA journey?

prepare.title: Prepare
prepare.tagline: The phase searchers underestimate — and the one that determines 80% of the outcome.
prepare.description:
Most searchers underestimate this phase, which determines 80% of the outcome. Preparation is where you decide what type of search fund matches your situation, which industries and geographies you’ll target, and whether you have the financial runway to spend 18–24 months on a potentially failed acquisition. According to the 2024 Stanford Search Fund Study, approximately 37% of searchers fail to acquire a company despite a dedicated two-year search — you need to be financially and psychologically prepared for this outcome.

During Prepare, you work on three parallel fronts. First, the personal dimension: runway, spouse alignment, career bridge if the search fails, and the mindset shift from salaried executive to founder-CEO with no paycheck. Second, the strategic dimension: which search fund model fits you (traditional, self-funded, single-sponsor, accelerator-backed), which industries align with your background, and what size of company you target (typically $1–3M EBITDA for first-time searchers). Third, the network dimension: building a circle of advisors before you even start — former searchers, accountants specializing in QoE reviews, M&A lawyers, and a handful of potential investors who have expressed interest.
prepare.quote: The right motivations for launching a search fund, in my view, are a trifecta of ambition, curiosity, and a keen interest in operational leadership. It offers more than just equity — it’s about being in charge from start to finish.
prepare.pitfall1: Underestimating the personal runway needed (18–24 months with no salary)
prepare.pitfall2: Choosing an industry you don’t understand deeply

fundraise.title: Fundraise
fundraise.tagline: Converting your thesis into capital and commitments.
fundraise.description:
The fundraising phase transforms your search thesis into committed capital. A typical traditional search fund raises $400,000–$600,000 from 10–20 investors, giving each a pro-rata right to invest in the eventual acquisition. The PPM (Private Placement Memorandum) is your business plan — it needs to communicate your industry thesis, search strategy, deal criteria, and fund economics clearly enough that investors can underwrite both you and your approach.

Investor selection matters as much as capital raised. Avoid single-investor dependency: if one LP controls more than 30% of your fund, they effectively control your search. Build a balanced cap table with investors who bring domain expertise, deal flow, and operating experience alongside their capital. The legal setup (typically a Delaware LP or LLC, or a Luxembourg SCSp for European funds) and shareholder agreements require M&A counsel experienced in search fund structures. The typical timeline is 4–6 months, not the 2 months many optimistic searchers plan for.
fundraise.quote: Relay was the first investor in my fund and has guided me through this process from fundraising, to searching, to acquiring and running my business. When COVID-19 hit in March 2020 and my deal was in jeopardy, they were my first call. I would not have been able to close my deal without that support.
fundraise.pitfall2: Accepting soft commitments as firm capital
fundraise.pitfall3: Poorly structured vesting that misaligns founder-investor incentives

search.title: Search
search.tagline: Where most of your time goes. Where most searches fail.
search.description:
The search phase is the longest and most psychologically demanding stage. You will evaluate hundreds of businesses to find the one worth acquiring. Deal sourcing splits into three channels: proprietary outreach (cold emails, cold calls, LinkedIn messages to business owners), intermediated deals (business brokers, M&A advisors), and network-generated leads (accountants, lawyers, wealth managers who advise business owners on succession).

Sector specialization dramatically improves your hit rate. Searchers who focus on 2–3 industries develop pattern recognition for what a good deal looks like, build deeper broker relationships, and can move faster on opportunities. Your pipeline should maintain 50–100 qualified leads at various stages. Most importantly, manage the emotional complexity: every seller is giving up their life’s work, and deal fatigue after months of near-misses can cloud your judgment on the deals that matter.
search.quote: I looked at hundreds, if not thousands of businesses. The search took me a year and eight months. Even if you run the best due diligence process that has ever been run, I strongly suspect you will learn more about your company in the first month inside it than in the eight months outside that preceded it.
search.pitfall1: Falling in love with the first decent target
search.pitfall2: Pipeline too concentrated in one sector or geography
search.pitfall3: Neglecting seller relationships after initial outreach

acquire.title: Acquire
acquire.tagline: Where the deal gets real — or falls apart.
acquire.description:
The acquisition phase compresses months of high-stakes work into a tight timeline. It begins with the Letter of Intent (LOI), which locks in key terms: purchase price, structure (asset vs. stock), financing contingencies, exclusivity period, and working capital peg. Once signed, you enter due diligence — financial, legal, commercial, and operational — running in parallel under time pressure.

The Quality of Earnings (QoE) report is your single most important diligence deliverable: it validates (or challenges) the seller’s EBITDA claims. Financing structure typically layers senior debt (40–60% of purchase price, often SBA 7(a) for US deals), equity from your investor group (30–50%), and a seller note (10–20%). SPA negotiation covers representations, warranties, indemnification caps and baskets, escrow amounts, and post-closing adjustments. Most deals that die in diligence fail because of surprises in working capital, customer concentration, or undisclosed liabilities — not because of price disagreements.
acquire.quote: We’ve never had a deal where we closed and not found something weird, hairy, unexpected after close, ever. It’s a form of humility to pay a lower price. It’s a form of humility not to use debt. And it’s a form of humility not to assume that you know what you can do with it post-close.
acquire.pitfall1: Incomplete or rushed due diligence under time pressure
acquire.pitfall2: Not having financing lined up before signing the LOI
acquire.pitfall3: Mis-pricing earnouts (too aggressive or too loose)

operate.title: Operate
operate.tagline: From deal-maker to operator. The longest phase, the one that creates the value.
operate.description:
The operate phase is where value is actually created — and where the searcher’s identity transforms from dealmaker to CEO. The first 100 days are critical: conduct a listening tour with every employee, customer, and supplier before making changes. Quick wins (fixing obvious inefficiencies, addressing long-neglected employee concerns, upgrading financial reporting) build credibility and buy time for larger strategic initiatives.

Employee retention is your biggest early risk — research shows 47% of employees leave within the first year after an acquisition. Build trust with the inherited management team before restructuring anything. As you settle in, focus shifts to growth: pricing optimization, sales team development, new customer channels, and potentially bolt-on acquisitions. Board governance with your investors becomes a regular operating rhythm — monthly financial packages, quarterly board meetings, and annual strategic reviews. This phase typically lasts 3–7 years, and it’s where the operator’s skill and persistence determine whether the investment returns 0x or 10x.
operate.quote: The importance of my own leadership. This was a shocker, but when you think you’re a great leader, it only means you haven’t set the bar high enough.
operate.pitfall1: Moving too fast on changes in the first 90 days
operate.pitfall2: Losing key employees the previous owner relied on
operate.pitfall3: Under-investing in the management team you inherited

exit.title: Exit
exit.tagline: Preparing for liquidity. Not always a sale — sometimes a recap, sometimes a hold.
exit.description:
Exit planning should begin 18–24 months before the target liquidity event. The primary exit routes are: strategic sale (to a corporate buyer paying a premium for synergies), financial sale (to a PE firm or another search fund), dividend recapitalization (refinancing to return capital while retaining ownership), or long-term hold (continuing to operate and distribute cash flows).

Preparing the company for sale means getting your house in order: audited or reviewed financials, a management team that can operate without you, documented processes, clean legal structure, and a compelling growth narrative. Vendor due diligence (preparing your own QoE report in advance) accelerates the sell-side process. Whether you hire an investment banker or run a direct process, a competitive auction typically yields 15–30% higher valuations than a negotiated single-buyer deal. Post-exit, distributions flow to investors per the waterfall in your original operating agreement, and you face the next question: another search, an investor role, or something entirely new.
exit.quote: The movie in Entrepreneur Land always ends with the exit, but there’s an epilogue. Life goes on, and finding your epilogue — figuring out how you create new structure, meaning, and identity — is what it’s all about.
exit.pitfall1: Waiting too long to prepare — exit prep should start 18–24 months before
exit.pitfall2: Not running a competitive process (leaving money on the table)
exit.pitfall3: Poorly structured earnouts that create post-close friction
